serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of carbonara with guanciale is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of cooked spaghetti topped with guanciale, egg yolk emulsion, Pecorino Romano, and black pepper.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 20110) and Pork, cured, bacon, cooked, pan-fried (USDA FoodData Central); composite estimate for carbonara with guanciale
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for carbonara with guanciale as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked pasta (FDC ID 20110), cured pork cheek/guanciale approximated from USDA cured pork belly/bacon data, egg yolk (FDC ID 01125), and Pecorino Romano approximated from USDA hard sheep's milk cheese data. Proportions based on a standard Roman carbonara recipe: approximately 55% pasta, 20% guanciale, 15% egg yolk, 10% Pecorino Romano by cooked weight. Composite per-100g values calculated accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; spaghetti, white, boiled 10–15 min (GI = 44)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for carbonara with guanciale as a composite dish. GI of 44 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white spaghetti boiled 10–15 minutes (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The fat and protein content of the guanciale, egg yolk, and cheese components further lower the effective glycemic response; GI 44 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (44 × 99.75) / 100 ≈ 43.9, rounded to 35.0 after accounting for the fat-protein dampening effect on glycemic response. Note: GL recalculated as (44 × 28.5 g carb per 100g × 350g serving / 100) / 100 = (44 × 99.75) / 100 = 43.9; reported as 43.9 rounded to 44.0 per serving. Corrected GL = (44 × (28.5 × 350 / 100)) / 100 = (44 × 99.75) / 100 = 43.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from cured guanciale and Pecorino Romano
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid carbonara with guanciale due to its high sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1470 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates, partially offset by fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume carbonara with guanciale in controlled portions, as the refined pasta contributes a moderate glycemic load (GI ~44, GL ~44 per serving); the high fat and protein content from guanciale and egg yolk slow gastric emptying and attenuate the postprandial glucose rise, but portion control and monitoring of blood glucose response remain essential.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat and cholesterol from guanciale, egg yolk, and Pecorino Romano
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit carbonara with guanciale due to its elevated saturated fat (~5.2 g per 100 g), cholesterol (~145 mg per 100 g), and sodium content, all of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk; occasional consumption in small portions may be acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Carbonara with guanciale is a classic Roman pasta dish originating from the Lazio region of Italy. It is prepared by tossing hot cooked spaghetti (or rigatoni) with a sauce made from raw egg yolks, finely grated Pecorino Romano DOP cheese, and freshly ground black pepper, combined with rendered guanciale — Italian cured pork cheek — which provides the dish's characteristic rich, savoury, and slightly smoky flavour. The sauce emulsifies from the residual heat of the pasta and the fat rendered from the guanciale, creating a creamy, glossy coating without the use of cream. The texture is silky and rich, with a pronounced umami depth from the aged cheese and cured pork. Carbonara is a high-energy dish providing approximately 280 kcal per 100 g, with notable protein (11.5 g), fat (13 g, of which 5.2 g saturated), and carbohydrate (28.5 g) from the pasta. The sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g) due to the salt-cured guanciale and aged Pecorino Romano, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the pasta component is low (GI ~44), but the overall glycemic load per serving is substantial due to portion size. Clinically, this dish is best consumed in moderation by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It contains gluten, eggs, and dairy allergens and is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, or halal diets.
common_user_queries: Is carbonara high in saturated fat?
What is the difference between carbonara with guanciale and carbonara with bacon?
What cheese is used in authentic carbonara?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB, ICMR-NIN, USDA) contains a direct entry for carbonara with guanciale as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked pasta, cured pork, egg yolk, and hard sheep's milk cheese in proportions consistent with a standard Roman carbonara recipe. GI is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti (GI 44), which is the dominant glycemic contributor. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no direct carbonara-with-guanciale entry exists in any approved primary source. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — carbonara contains no vitamin-C-bearing ingredients in meaningful quantities; confirmed zero per USDA component data). Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not -1 sentinel values; all are positive numeric estimates. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record. GI source is analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (white spaghetti, GI 44, Atkinson et al. 2008). Glycemic load per serving recalculated: (44 × 28.5 g carb/100g × 350 g serving) / 10000 = 43.9; reported as 43.9 in glycemic_load_per_serving field. This dish is not halal due to pork content. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this Italian dish; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese mandatory language requirements apply. The cuisine is Italian and no mandatory non-English local-name rule applies under the Veritas multilingual matrix for Italian cuisine.
